summary: Gruber highlights the absurdity of Microsoft's MSN music executive Yusuf Mehdi claiming their music service was not a response to Apple's iTunes Music Store. He points to Microsoft naming their newsletter 'Music Tuesday' — the same name concept as Apple's 'iTunes Tuesday' — as evidence of direct imitation. After readers correct him that Tuesday is the industry-standard release day, Gruber pivots to the stronger point: Mehdi's own subsequent answers contradict his initial claim, admitting Apple launched before Microsoft had even considered the idea and that the decision to sell music was only made a year prior.
key_points: Microsoft's MSN music VP claimed their music service was independently conceived and not a response to iTunes Music Store
The naming similarity between Microsoft's and Apple's Tuesday-themed music newsletters suggests direct imitation rather than independent development
Mehdi's own interview answers contradict each other — first claiming independence, then admitting Apple launched before Microsoft even considered the idea
Microsoft's pattern of following Apple's lead while publicly denying it is presented as both transparent and faintly ridiculous
money_quotes: No, of course it's not in response to the iTunes Music Store.
I mean, what's Microsoft supposed to do? Release their new music on *Wednesdays*?
My 'Release their new music on Wednesdays?' crack about the day of the week is off-base, but come on — the *exact* same newsletter title?
Apple had already gotten out before we had even thought about doing our own particular service.
Both of which answers seem to contradict the first, that they were 'on a path already to provide great music services' before the ITMS.
key_insight: Microsoft's executive couldn't even maintain the fiction of independent innovation within a single interview, with his own answers revealing that their music service was plainly a reaction to Apple's iTunes Music Store.
